title: Distribution Shape & Position
blurb: Histograms and shape, percentiles, z-scores, and the normal curve with the 68–95–99.7 rule.

lessons.title: Histograms: Seeing the Whole Distribution
lessons.concreteComparison: A spreadsheet of $200$ commute times is unreadable — a wall of numbers. Chop the time axis into $10$-minute bins, count how many commutes land in each, and draw a bar per bin: suddenly you SEE it — a hump at $20$–$30$ minutes, a straggle of bad days stretching right. The histogram is how a dataset becomes a shape.
lessons.objective: Build and read histograms: choose bins, count frequencies, and extract shape, center, and spread by eye.
concept: A **histogram** bins a numeric axis into equal-width intervals and draws each bin's COUNT (frequency) as a bar — bars touch, because the axis is continuous; a gap means an empty bin, not a design choice.

**Bin width matters:** too wide smears detail into a blob; too narrow shatters the shape into noise. Useful default: $5$–$12$ bins for classroom-size data; always try a second width before trusting a shape.
**Reading**: the hump(s) locate the center; the horizontal stretch shows spread; tails announce skew; separated humps announce mixed populations (Unit 9's bimodal warning, now visible).
Convention: a value on a bin edge goes in the RIGHT bin (edge-inclusive on the left: $[20, 30)$) — state the rule once and count consistently, or edge values get double-counted.
lessons.keyIdea: Equal-width bins, touching bars, counts as heights — the histogram turns a number wall into shape, center, and spread you can see.

facts.title: Anatomy
facts.explanation: Equal bins, touching bars; gaps are empty bins.

facts.title: Bin discipline
facts.explanation: Edge values counted once, by a stated rule.

workedExamples.statement: A histogram of $50$ house prices shows bars (counts): $3, 12, 18, 10, 4$, then a lone bar of $3$ far to the right. Describe the distribution.
workedExamples.solution: Main body: single hump (center in the third bin), roughly symmetric core of $47$ homes. The detached right bar: $3$ luxury properties — a separated cluster, likely flag-worthy. Report: "unimodal core with $3$ high outliers"; median + IQR for the headline (Unit 9's pairing).

workedExamples.statement: The same $200$ commutes are binned two ways: $4$ bins (shape: one smooth hump) and $40$ bins (shape: jagged spikes). Which do you trust, and what's the fix?
workedExamples.solution: Neither extreme: $4$ bins smears (could hide bimodality), $40$ shatters ($5$ per bin — noise). Fix: middle widths ($8$–$12$ bins), and CHECK ROBUSTNESS — a real feature (second hump, gap) survives several widths; an artifact appears in one and vanishes in the next.

commonMistakes.text: Leaving gaps between bars of adjacent bins.
commonMistakes.correction: Bars touch — the axis is continuous. Gapped bars belong to bar CHARTS of categories (colors, brands), a different tool for different data.

commonMistakes.text: Trusting the shape from one bin width.
commonMistakes.correction: Shapes can be artifacts of binning. Redraw at a second width; only features that survive are real.

tryIt.solution: Counts: $2, 5, 2, 1$ (sum $10$ ✓). Single hump in $[5,10)$, tail stretching right: right-skewed.

tryIt.statement: A histogram shows two clearly separated humps of adult heights at a family reunion. What's the statistical story?
tryIt.solution: Bimodal — two mixed populations (likely adults vs children, or two age groups). Split before summarizing; one mean would land between the humps and describe nobody.

steps.title: From wall of numbers to shape
beats: $200$ commute times as a list: unreadable.
Chop the axis into bins. Count per bin. Bar per count.
Suddenly: a hump at $20$–$30$ min, a right tail of bad days.
The histogram is the dataset's portrait.

steps.title: Bins and bars
beats: Equal widths. Touching bars (continuous axis!).
Edge rule: $[20, 30)$ — left-inclusive, stated once, applied always.
Too few bins smear; too many shatter. Try two widths.
Gaps mean EMPTY bins — sometimes the most informative feature.

steps.title: Same scores, three histograms
steps.teach: Twenty-seven test scores. Width 25 hides everything; width 5 shows texture; width 10 shows the shape cleanly. A histogram is a **choice**, not a fact — the data never changes, only the grouping. Always ask what bin width you're looking at before you trust the shape.

steps.title: Where does 30 go?
steps.prompt: With bins $[20, 30)$ and $[30, 40)$, the value $30$ is counted in…
steps.explanation: $[20, 30)$ excludes its right edge; $[30, 40)$ includes its left. One rule, one count — double-counting edges is how bin totals stop matching $n$.

steps.title: Diagnose from bars
steps.prompt: A histogram's bars climb slowly from the left and drop sharply at the right end (exam scores with a max of 100). The shape is…
options: Left-skewed — long low tail, pile-up near the top
steps.explanation: The TAIL stretches left (weak scores), the crowd piles near 100 — left/negative skew, the signature of an easy exam or a ceiling effect. Skew names the tail (Unit 9).

steps.title: Histogram ≠ bar chart
steps.body: Histograms bin a CONTINUOUS number line (bars touch, order is fixed by the axis). Bar charts count CATEGORIES (bars gap, order is yours). Using one where the other belongs is the most common chart crime in student reports — and newspapers.

steps.title: Florence Nightingale, chartmaker
steps.body: Nightingale's 1858 mortality diagrams — proto-histograms of soldier deaths by cause and month — convinced the British government that sanitation, not battle, was the killer. She's credited as the first person to use statistical graphics to drive public policy. A well-binned chart has been saving lives ever since.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Equal bins, touching bars, one edge rule, counts sum to n.
Shape by eye: humps (center), stretch (spread), tails (skew), gaps (clusters).
Try two bin widths; only surviving features are real.

lessons.title: Percentiles: Position in the Crowd
lessons.concreteComparison: "Your baby is in the $85$th percentile for height." No parent hears a formula, but every parent understands it: taller than $85\%$ of babies. Raw numbers ($74$ cm? is that a lot?) mean nothing without a crowd to stand in — the percentile IS your position in the crowd.
lessons.objective: Compute percentile ranks from data, interpret percentile statements, and connect quartiles to the percentile scale.
concept: The **percentile rank** of a value is the percentage of the data at or below it: $$\text{rank} = \frac{\#\{\text{values} \le x\}}{n} \times 100\%$$ (Conventions vary — some count strictly below; state yours and stay consistent.)
The landmarks you already own translate directly: median $=$ 50th percentile, $Q_1 = $ 25th, $Q_3 = $ 75th. The five-number summary is five percentile statements.

Percentiles measure POSITION, not quality gaps: moving from the 50th to the 60th percentile in a dense crowd takes a whisker; from the 90th to the 99th, in a thin tail, takes a leap. Equal percentile steps are unequal skill steps.

Percentiles compare across different scales: an $85$th-percentile math score and an $85$th-percentile sprint time are equally exceptional in their own crowds — the crowd, not the units, carries the meaning.
lessons.keyIdea: Percentile rank = share of the crowd at or below you; quartiles are the 25/50/75 landmarks; equal percentile steps ≠ equal skill steps.

facts.title: Percentile rank
facts.explanation: Your position as a share of the crowd (at-or-below convention).

facts.title: The landmarks
facts.explanation: Unit 9's summary, restated on the percentile scale.

workedExamples.statement: Class scores (sorted): $45, 52, 58, 61, 64, 68, 71, 75, 82, 90$. Find the percentile rank of the student who scored $75$.
workedExamples.solution: Values at or below $75$: eight of ten. Rank $= \frac{8}{10} \times 100 = 80$th percentile — ahead of (or level with) $80\%$ of the class.

workedExamples.statement: In the same class, what score sits at the 25th percentile, and what's its Unit 9 name?
workedExamples.solution: $25\%$ of $10$ values $= 2.5 \to$ the boundary falls after the lower quarter; by our convention the lower half $\{45,52,58,61,64\}$ has median $58$: $Q_1 = 58$, the first quartile — the 25th percentile's older name.

workedExamples.statement: Ana moved from the 50th to the 60th percentile on a national exam; Bat moved from the 89th to the 99th. Same 10-point percentile gain — same improvement?
workedExamples.solution: No. Around the median the crowd is DENSE — a small score bump leapfrogs many people. At the top the crowd thins — gaining the last $10$ percentile points means overtaking the strongest tenth, usually a far larger skill jump. Percentiles measure position; the score-to-percentile exchange rate varies along the scale.

commonMistakes.text: Reading the 85th percentile as a score of 85%.
commonMistakes.correction: Percentile is position, not score: you can hit the 85th percentile with 60% correct on a hard exam. The crowd, not the test, defines it.

commonMistakes.text: Treating percentile steps as equal-sized skill steps.
commonMistakes.correction: 50→60 and 89→99 are both '10 points' of position but vastly different feats — crowd density varies along the scale.

tryIt.statement: Sorted times of $8$ swimmers: your friend is $6$th fastest — i.e., $6$th from the top, $3$rd from the bottom... compute her percentile among times where LOWER is better, counting at-or-below her time as $3$ of $8$.
tryIt.solution: Rank $= \frac{3}{8} \times 100 = 37.5$th percentile of slowness — better stated: she's faster than $62.5\%$... careful with direction! With times, decide first whether the scale ranks speed or slowness, then count.

tryIt.statement: A standardized test reports your 90th percentile among $2400$ test-takers. About how many scored at or below you?

steps.title: Numbers need crowds
beats: "$74$ cm tall" — is that a lot? For a baby? Which age?
"$85$th percentile" — instantly clear: taller than $85\%$ of the crowd.
Position in the crowd IS the information.
Rank $= \frac{\text{at or below}}{n} \times 100\%$.

steps.title: Count the crowd
steps.prompt: Sorted data of $20$ values; Tulga's value is $14$th from the bottom. His percentile rank (at-or-below)?
steps.explanation: $\frac{14}{20} \times 100 = 70$. The raw position ($14$th) isn't a percentile until divided by the crowd; $30$th counts from the wrong end.

steps.title: Quartiles are percentiles
beats: The five-number summary: percentiles $0, 25, 50, 75, 100$.
Growth charts, test reports, income tables — all percentile landmarks.
One scale, every crowd.

steps.title: The doctor's sentence
steps.prompt: "Your baby is 85th percentile for height, 40th for weight." This means…
options: Taller than 85% and lighter than 60% of peers — a tall, lean baby
The baby scored 85 and 40 on two tests
steps.explanation: Two positions in two crowds: height near the top, weight just below the middle — a description, not a diagnosis. Percentiles LOCATE; doctors (and context) interpret.

steps.title: Know which way is 'good'
steps.body: For scores, higher percentile = better. For race times, cholesterol, and latency, LOWER values are better — so a '90th percentile response time' is bad news. Before interpreting any percentile, ask: on this scale, which direction is the compliment?

steps.title: Growth charts are fifty crowds deep
steps.body: Pediatric growth-chart percentiles come from decades of measured children, re-standardized by the WHO in 2006 using healthy breastfed infants across six countries — so 'the crowd' is now a global reference. When your doctor says '85th percentile', your baby is being compared with children from Accra to Oslo.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Percentile rank = at-or-below share × 100; quartiles are the 25/50/75 landmarks.
Position, not score — and the score-to-percentile exchange rate varies.
Check the scale's direction before congratulating anyone.

lessons.title: Z-Scores: The Universal Ruler
lessons.concreteComparison: Ana scored $85$ on a math exam (class mean $70$, SD $10$); Bat scored $700$ on a national test (mean $500$, SD $100$). Who did better? Different scales — same trick: measure each score in STANDARD DEVIATIONS from its own mean. Ana: $\frac{85-70}{10} = 1.5$ SDs up. Bat: $\frac{700-500}{100} = 2.0$ SDs up. Bat wins, on the only ruler both tests share.
lessons.objective: Standardize values with z = (x − mean)/SD, interpret z-scores, and compare across different distributions.
concept: The **z-score** converts any value to SD-units: $$z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{s}$$ — "how many standard deviations from the mean, and in which direction." Positive = above the mean, negative = below, zero = exactly average.
Z-scores are UNIT-FREE: the centimeters, points, or seconds cancel in the division. That's what makes cross-scale comparisons legal — every distribution gets the same ruler.
Working benchmarks: $|z| < 1$ is unremarkable, $|z| \approx 2$ is edge-of-usual (Unit 8's $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ rule speaking), $|z| > 3$ is rare in bell-shaped data and worth an investigation anywhere.
Z-scores run BACKWARD too: $x = \bar{x} + z \cdot s$ recovers the raw value — "the cutoff is $z = 1.5$" becomes a real score once you know the mean and SD.
lessons.keyIdea: z = (x − mean)/SD: distance from the mean in SD units — unit-free, sign-carrying, and reversible via x = mean + z·SD.

facts.title: Standardize
facts.explanation: SDs above (+) or below (−) the mean — the universal ruler.

facts.title: Un-standardize
facts.explanation: Z-cutoffs convert back to raw scores with the distribution's own mean and SD.

workedExamples.statement: Ana: $85$ on a test with mean $70$, SD $10$. Bat: $700$ on a test with mean $500$, SD $100$. Standardize both and compare.
workedExamples.solution: $z_{Ana} = \frac{85 - 70}{10} = 1.5$; $z_{Bat} = \frac{700 - 500}{100} = 2.0$. Bat stands further above his crowd — the better relative performance, even though the raw numbers can't be compared at all.

workedExamples.statement: Heights: mean $170$ cm, SD $8$. Find the z-scores of a $154$ cm and a $190$ cm person, and interpret.
workedExamples.solution: $z = \frac{154 - 170}{8} = -2$: two SDs BELOW — edge of usual, notably short in this crowd. $z = \frac{190 - 170}{8} = 2.5$: unusually tall, investigation-of-the-basketball-team territory.

workedExamples.statement: A scholarship requires $z \ge 1.8$ on an exam with mean $62$ and SD $9$. Find the raw cutoff score.
workedExamples.solution: Backward: $x = 62 + 1.8 \times 9 = 62 + 16.2 = 78.2$ — students need about $79$ (next attainable score at or above the cutoff).

commonMistakes.correction: z = −2 and z = +2 are equally UNUSUAL but opposite stories. The sign is half the information; 'two SDs away' without direction is incomplete.

commonMistakes.text: Comparing raw scores across different scales.
commonMistakes.correction: 85 vs 700 compares nothing. Standardize first — z-scores are the exchange rate between distributions.

tryIt.statement: Mean $50$, SD $4$: find the z-scores of $58$ and $44$.
A dataset has mean $200$ and SD $25$. What raw value sits at $z = -1.2$?

steps.title: 85 vs 700
beats: Raw comparison: meaningless. Different tests, different scales.
Shared ruler: SDs from the own-crowd mean.
Ana $+1.5$, Bat $+2.0$ — Bat wins, fairly.

steps.title: Subtract the mean, divide by the SD
beats: $z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{s}$ — position minus center, in spread-units.
Units CANCEL: cm, points, seconds all become 'SDs'.
Sign carries direction: $+$ above, $-$ below, $0$ dead average.
Benchmarks: $|z|<1$ ordinary, $\approx 2$ eyebrow, $>3$ investigate.

steps.title: Distance, measured in sigmas
steps.teach: Class scores: $\mu = 70$, $\sigma = 8$. Step the marker to 82 and the readout does the conversion: $z = \frac{82 - 70}{8} = 1.5$ — a score one-and-a-half standard deviations above the mean. The z-score is a change of units: from points to **sigmas**, which is what makes scores from different scales comparable.

steps.title: Standardize
steps.prompt: Mean $80$, SD $5$: the z-score of $72$ is…
steps.explanation: $\frac{72 - 80}{5} = -1.6$ — below the mean, so NEGATIVE. Dropping the sign (option 2) loses the direction; $-8$ forgot to divide by the SD.

steps.title: Which feat is rarer?
steps.prompt: A runner's $100$m time is $z = -2.5$ (lower = faster) in her league; a chess player's rating is $z = +1.8$ in his club. Whose performance stands further from typical?
options: The chess player — positive beats negative
steps.explanation: Distance from typical is $|z|$: $2.5$ SDs beats $1.8$, whatever the sport. (The runner's NEGATIVE z is the good direction for times.) Unit-free is the whole point — z-scores compare anything to anything.

steps.title: Speak z in sentences
steps.body: Never leave a z-score naked. "z = 1.5" becomes "one and a half SDs above the class average — good but not exceptional." The translation forces the sign, the benchmark, and the crowd into view — where the meaning lives.

steps.title: IQ is a z-score in costume
steps.body: IQ scores are literally z-scores rescaled to mean 100 and SD 15: an IQ of 130 IS z = +2. SAT sections (mean ~500, SD ~100), T-scores in psychology (mean 50, SD 10) — the same standardization wearing different uniforms. Learn one ruler, read them all.

steps.title: What you learned
points: z = (x − mean)/SD: unit-free distance-with-direction from the mean.
Backward: x = mean + z·SD turns cutoffs into raw scores.

lessons.title: The Normal Curve & the Empirical Rule
lessons.concreteComparison: Heights, measurement errors, blood pressures, exam scores, the Galton board's bean piles from Unit 4 — histogram after histogram keeps producing the same silhouette: one symmetric hump, tails fading evenly. Nature reuses this shape so relentlessly it earned the name **normal** — and for it, the vague benchmarks of the last lesson sharpen into exact percentages: $68$–$95$–$99.7$.
lessons.objective: Recognize the normal distribution, and apply the empirical rule: 68% within 1 SD, 95% within 2, 99.7% within 3.
concept: The **normal distribution** is the symmetric bell: centered at its mean ($=$ median), spread set by its SD, tails thinning fast and never quite touching zero. Two numbers — $\mu$ and $\sigma$ — pin down the entire curve.
**The empirical rule** (68–95–99.7): about $68\%$ of values lie within $1$ SD of the mean, $95\%$ within $2$, $99.7\%$ within $3$. Unit 8's 'usual range' was this rule, rounded.

Symmetry slices the rule into tail-pieces: outside $\pm 1$ SD lives $32\%$ — $16\%$ per tail. Outside $\pm 2$: $5\%$ — $2.5\%$ per tail. Between $1$ and $2$ SDs above: $\frac{95 - 68}{2} = 13.5\%$. Sketch, slice, add.

WHY nature loves the bell: sums of many small independent influences (genes, measurement jitters, coin flips) smooth toward it — the Central Limit Theorem's shadow, seen already in the binomial's hill (Unit 8). Skewed data (incomes!) is NOT normal — check shape before applying the rule.
lessons.keyIdea: The bell is set by μ and σ alone; 68–95–99.7 within 1–2–3 SDs, sliced by symmetry into tail percentages.

facts.title: The empirical rule
facts.explanation: The bell's fixed proportions — valid for normal-shaped data only.

facts.title: Tail arithmetic
facts.explanation: Symmetry splits what's outside each band into two equal tails.

workedExamples.statement: Adult heights: normal with mean $170$ cm, SD $8$. What share of adults stands between $162$ and $178$ cm? Between $154$ and $186$?
Same heights: what share is TALLER than $186$ cm?
workedExamples.solution: $186 = \mu + 2\sigma$. Outside $\pm 2\sigma$: $5\%$, split by symmetry: $2.5\%$ in the upper tail. One adult in forty.

workedExamples.statement: Exam scores: normal, mean $72$, SD $6$. What share scored between $78$ and $84$?
workedExamples.solution: $78 = \mu + 1\sigma$, $84 = \mu + 2\sigma$: the band from $1$ to $2$ SDs above. Within $2$: $95\%$; within $1$: $68\%$; the difference $27\%$ splits between the two sides: $13.5\%$ on the upper side.

commonMistakes.correction: The rule belongs to the BELL. Incomes, waiting times, house prices are skewed — their 1-SD band holds a different share. Check the shape (histogram!) before quoting the rule.
5% lies outside ±2σ — but only 2.5% in EACH tail. 'Above +2σ' is half the outside, always.

tryIt.statement: Normal: mean $100$, SD $15$. What share lies between $85$ and $115$? Above $130$?
Battery life: normal, mean $40$ h, SD $3$. Between what two lifetimes do the middle $99.7\%$ of batteries fall?

steps.title: Nature's favorite silhouette
beats: Heights. Errors. Blood pressure. Bean-machine piles (Unit 4!).
Same portrait every time: symmetric hump, fading tails.
Two numbers pin the whole curve: $\mu$ (center) and $\sigma$ (width).
It earned the name NORMAL by showing up everywhere.
Unit 8's 'usual range' was this rule, rounded.
Symmetry slices the rest: $16\%$ per outer-1σ tail, $2.5\%$ per outer-2σ.
Sketch the bell, mark the bands, add the slices. Every problem.

steps.title: 68 — 95 — 99.7, on the axis
steps.teach: A normal distribution with $\mu = 100$, $\sigma = 15$. Tap the bands: about 68% of values land within one sigma (85 to 115), 95% within two (70 to 130), 99.7% within three. Those three numbers are the empirical rule — the whole reason knowing $\mu$ and $\sigma$ is knowing almost everything about a normal.

steps.title: One band
steps.prompt: Normal, mean $60$, SD $5$: what share lies between $55$ and $65$?
steps.explanation: $55$–$65$ is $\mu \pm 1\sigma$: the $68\%$ band. The bell concentrates two-thirds of everything within one SD — the single most useful fact about it.

steps.title: Tails and bands
steps.prompt: For a normal distribution, the share BELOW $\mu - 1\sigma$ is…
steps.explanation: Outside $\pm 1\sigma$: $32\%$; the lower tail takes half: $16\%$. Forgetting to halve is the classic slip — sketch the bell and the halving draws itself.

steps.title: Shape check before rule use
steps.body: The empirical rule is a property of the BELL, not of data in general. One glance at the histogram first: symmetric single hump → rule applies; skew or two humps → it doesn't, and quoting it anyway produces confident nonsense. (For skewed data, quartiles still work — they're shape-agnostic.)

steps.title: The curve on the money
steps.body: Germany's old 10-Deutsche-Mark note carried Carl Friedrich Gauss's portrait AND the normal curve with its formula — possibly the only probability density ever printed on currency. The 'Gaussian' turns up in GPS error, image blur, and stock models; the Bundesbank judged it literally worth money.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Normal: symmetric bell, fully set by μ and σ.
68–95–99.7 within 1–2–3 SDs; symmetry halves the outsides.
Shape-check first: the rule belongs to bells, not to all data.

lessons.title: Working the Bell
lessons.concreteComparison: A machine fills juice bottles: normal, mean $503$ ml, SD $2$. The label promises $500$ ml — what fraction of bottles break the promise? $500$ is $z = -1.5$... between the landmarks. Real problems land between the 68–95–99.7 posts, and the workflow — standardize, sketch, slice — handles the landmarks exactly and everything else by honest bracketing.
lessons.objective: Solve normal-distribution problems end to end: standardize, use the empirical rule at landmarks, and bracket between them.
concept: The universal workflow: (1) STANDARDIZE the value(s) to z-scores; (2) SKETCH the bell and shade the asked-for region; (3) SLICE with the empirical rule — exact at $z \in \{1, 2, 3\}$, brackets between.

Bracketing is honest and often sufficient: $z = -1.5$ lies between the $16\%$ (at $-1$) and $2.5\%$ (at $-2$) tail marks — "between $2.5\%$ and $16\%$, nearer the middle" decides most practical questions. (Precise values come from tables/calculators — the workflow stays identical.)

Backward problems give the SHARE and ask the VALUE: "the top $2.5\%$" sits above $z = 2$, so the cutoff is $\mu + 2\sigma$. Percentile language maps straight to landmarks: median $= z_0$... the $84$th percentile $= z_1$, the $97.5$th $= z_2$.

Quality-control framing ties the course together: a spec limit at $z = -1.5$ means a $\sim 7\%$ violation rate per bottle — and Unit 8's binomial then counts violations per crate. The distributions stack.
lessons.keyIdea: Standardize → sketch → slice: exact at the 1-2-3 landmarks, bracket between them, and run backward from shares to cutoffs via z.

facts.title: The workflow
facts.explanation: Standardize, draw the bell, apply the rule at or between landmarks.

facts.title: Percentile landmarks
facts.explanation: The empirical rule, restated as percentiles.

workedExamples.statement: Bottles: normal, mean $503$ ml, SD $2$; label promises $500$ ml. Bracket the fraction of under-filled bottles.
workedExamples.solution: $z = \frac{500 - 503}{2} = -1.5$: between the landmarks. Below $-1$: $16\%$; below $-2$: $2.5\%$. The under-fill rate is between $2.5\%$ and $16\%$ — roughly $7\%$ by the midpoint of the bracket. (Exact tables give $6.7\%$; the bracket already told the story.)

workedExamples.statement: Test scores: normal, mean $250$, SD $40$. A program admits the top $16\%$. Find the cutoff score.
workedExamples.solution: Top $16\%$ begins at $z = +1$ (the upper-tail landmark). Cutoff: $250 + 1 \times 40 = 290$.

workedExamples.statement: Reaction times: normal, mean $300$ ms, SD $20$. A gamer clocks $260$ ms (lower = better). What percentile is that, and how rare?
workedExamples.solution: $z = \frac{260 - 300}{20} = -2$: two SDs on the GOOD side. Share faster: the below-$-2\sigma$ tail, $2.5\%$ — she's at the $2.5$th percentile of times, i.e., faster than $97.5\%$ of players. One in forty.

commonMistakes.correction: The empirical rule knows 1, 2, 3 SDs — nothing in between. At z = −1.5, bracket (2.5%–16%) or use a table; quoting '9.3%' from the rule alone is fabricated precision.

commonMistakes.text: Losing the direction in backward problems.
commonMistakes.correction: 'Top 16%' sits ABOVE +1σ; 'bottom 16%' below −1σ. Sketch the bell and shade BEFORE computing — the picture prevents mirrored cutoffs.

tryIt.statement: Normal: mean $75$, SD $5$. What share scores above $85$, and what percentile is a score of $70$?
tryIt.solution: $85$ is $z = +2$: above it, $2.5\%$. $70$ is $z = -1$: the $16$th percentile.

tryIt.statement: Lightbulb lifetimes: normal, mean $1200$ h, SD $50$. The maker wants to guarantee a lifetime that $97.5\%$ of bulbs exceed. What guarantee?

steps.title: Standardize, sketch, slice
beats: Every normal problem, three moves:
1 — Convert to z. 2 — Sketch the bell, SHADE the question.
3 — Slice: exact at $z = 1, 2, 3$; bracket in between.
The sketch isn't decoration — it's error-proofing.

steps.title: Heights under the bell
steps.teach: Adult heights: $\mu = 170$ cm, $\sigma = 7$ cm. The ±1σ band says 68% of people stand between 163 and 177 cm; ±2σ says 95% between 156 and 184. Every applied normal problem is this move: translate the question onto the axis, then read the band.

steps.title: Between the posts
steps.prompt: Normal data, and a value at $z = 2.4$. The share above it is…
steps.explanation: $2.4$ sits between the $z=2$ ($2.5\%$ above) and $z=3$ ($0.15\%$ above) landmarks: bracket it. The rule alone can't produce $1.7\%$ — that needs a table; claiming it from 68-95-99.7 is invented precision.

steps.title: Share to cutoff
steps.prompt: Normal: mean $500$, SD $100$. The bottom $2.5\%$ ends at…
steps.explanation: Bottom $2.5\%$ = below $z = -2$: $500 - 200 = 300$. The $700$ mirrors the tail to the wrong side — the sketch-and-shade step exists precisely to catch that.

steps.title: The bell feeds the binomial
steps.body: One bottle under-fills with p ≈ 0.07 (normal reasoning). A crate holds 12 — how many under-filled per crate? That's binomial(12, 0.07): Unit 8 consuming Unit 10's output. Real quality control stacks distributions exactly this way; you now own the full pipeline.

steps.title: Six Sigma's name is a z-score
steps.body: The manufacturing philosophy 'Six Sigma' names a goal: spec limits six SDs from the process mean, making defects astronomically rare (about 2 per billion at the pure math; ~3.4 per million with the industry's drift allowance). Entire corporations reorganized around pushing |z| of failure to 6. The ruler you learned this week runs factories.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Workflow: standardize → sketch & shade → slice or bracket.
Backward: shares map to z-landmarks, then x = μ + zσ.
Bell output feeds binomial input — distributions stack.

lessons.title: Capstone: Locating Anything
lessons.concreteComparison: A university gets two applicants: Ana with $86$ on Exam A (mean $70$, SD $8$) and Bat with $640$ on Exam B (mean $520$, SD $80$, both roughly normal). Same major, one seat. The admissions officer's whole job is this unit: standardize both, translate to percentiles, sanity-check the shapes, and defend the pick in plain language. You can now do that job.
lessons.objective: Combine histograms, percentiles, z-scores, and the empirical rule to locate and compare values across any distributions.
concept: The full location toolkit: HISTOGRAM to check shape (is the bell machinery even legal?), Z-SCORE to standardize position, EMPIRICAL RULE to convert z to shares at landmarks, PERCENTILE to speak position in crowd language.

The comparison pipeline: shape-check both distributions → standardize both values → compare $|z|$'s (or convert to percentiles for the memo). Different scales, different units, different crowds — one ruler.

Percentiles and z-scores are twin languages for the same fact: in normal data, $z = 1 \leftrightarrow P_{84}$, $z = 2 \leftrightarrow P_{97.5}$. Technical audiences take z; parents, patients, and памятlets take percentiles. Translate freely.

The honest caveats, always attached: z-comparisons assume both distributions are roughly symmetric (skew breaks the z-to-percentile bridge); and position measures RELATIVE standing — an 84th-percentile score in a weak cohort may lag a 60th in a strong one. Statistics locates; judgment decides.
lessons.keyIdea: Shape-check, standardize, slice, translate to percentiles — one pipeline locates any value in any crowd, with caveats attached.

facts.title: The twin languages
facts.explanation: Same position, two dialects — technical (z) and public (percentile).

facts.title: The pipeline
facts.explanation: Check, standardize, slice, translate — with caveats stated.

workedExamples.statement: Ana: $86$ on Exam A ($\mu = 70$, $\sigma = 8$). Bat: $640$ on Exam B ($\mu = 520$, $\sigma = 80$). Both roughly normal. Run the full pipeline.
workedExamples.solution: Shapes: both normal (stated) — machinery legal. Standardize: $z_{Ana} = \frac{86-70}{8} = 2.0$; $z_{Bat} = \frac{640-520}{80} = 1.5$. Slices: Ana at $P_{97.5}$ (top $2.5\%$); Bat at $\approx P_{93}$ (between $P_{84}$ and $P_{97.5}$). Memo: "Both excellent; Ana stands in her exam's top fortieth, Bat in roughly his top fifteenth — Ana by position."

workedExamples.statement: A clinic flags patients outside $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ on a blood measure (normal, $\mu = 120$, $\sigma = 10$). What share gets flagged, and what are the flag thresholds?
workedExamples.solution: Thresholds: $100$ and $140$. Outside $\pm 2\sigma$: $5\%$ of healthy patients flagged ($2.5\%$ each side) — the false-alarm rate BUILT INTO the cutoff choice. (Unit 6's base-rate lesson: most flags in a healthy population are false alarms. The units keep joining hands.)

workedExamples.statement: Bat's coach protests: "Exam B's cohort was national-level; Ana's was one school. The percentiles aren't comparable!" Is the protest statistically sound?
workedExamples.solution: Yes — and it's the unit's built-in caveat: percentiles measure standing WITHIN a crowd, and unequal crowds break cross-crowd comparisons. The fix is a common yardstick (both take the same exam, or link the exams statistically). Position tools are exact about relative standing and silent about absolute skill; knowing which question you've answered is the mark of statistical maturity.

commonMistakes.text: Comparing z-scores without checking the crowds are comparable.
commonMistakes.correction: z locates you within YOUR crowd. If cohorts differ in strength, equal z's mean different absolute performances — state the caveat or equalize the crowds.

commonMistakes.text: Using the z-to-percentile bridge on skewed data.
commonMistakes.correction: z = 1 ↔ P84 is a BELL fact. In skewed data, compute percentiles directly from the sorted values — the bridge is out.

tryIt.statement: Chess ratings in a club: normal, mean $1500$, SD $120$. Translate a rating of $1740$ into a z-score and a percentile.
tryIt.solution: $z = \frac{1740 - 1500}{120} = 2$: the $97.5$th percentile — top $2.5\%$ of the club.

tryIt.statement: Two friends compare gym numbers: Dulguun benches at $z = +1.2$ among men his age; Saraa deadlifts at $z = +1.9$ among women hers. Who's more exceptional, and what's the honest caveat?
tryIt.solution: Saraa — further above her crowd's mean ($1.9 > 1.2$ SDs). Caveat: each is located within their OWN reference population; the comparison is of relative standing, not of kilograms.

steps.title: One seat, two exams
beats: Ana: $86$ on Exam A. Bat: $640$ on Exam B. One seat.
The admissions job IS this unit:
shape-check → standardize → slice → translate → defend in plain words.
Run the pipeline.

steps.title: Twin languages
steps.prompt: A normal-data value at $z = +1$ sits at which percentile?
steps.explanation: Below $+1\sigma$: the $50\%$ lower half plus the $34\%$ half-band $= 84\%$. ($P_{68}$ misreads the whole band as one-sided.) z and percentile are dialects of one fact.

steps.title: When z lies
steps.prompt: Which comparison is statistically UNSAFE without extra work?
options: z-comparing percentiles from a strong cohort vs a weak cohort
z-comparing two students in the SAME class
translating z = 2 to P97.5 on bell-shaped data
steps.explanation: Cross-cohort z's compare relative standings in DIFFERENT crowds — fine to state, unsafe to treat as absolute skill. Same-crowd comparisons and bell-legal translations are exactly what the tools promise.

steps.title: Locate, then relate
steps.body: Units 9–10 describe and locate SINGLE variables. But the juiciest questions relate TWO: does studying predict scores? Height and weight? Ads and sales? Unit 11: scatterplots, correlation, and the line of best fit — where data grows a second dimension.

steps.title: Grading on a curve is this lesson
steps.body: 'Curved' exams literally impose a bell: professors map raw scores to z-scores, then to letter-grade bands (top 2.5% get A+, next 13.5% A...). The controversial part isn't the math — it's the assumption that every cohort deserves the same grade distribution. Now you can argue both sides with the actual machinery.

steps.title: What you learned — Unit 10 complete
points: Pipeline: shape-check → z → slice → percentile, caveats attached.
z and percentiles: twin dialects; the bridge needs the bell.
Position is relative to its crowd — equal crowds or explicit caveats. Next: two variables.

practice.solution: Counts: $2, 4, 3, 1$ — sum $10$ ✓. Single hump, mild right tail.

practice.statement: In a sorted list of $40$ values, Nomin's value is $30$th from the bottom. Find her percentile rank.
Mean $64$, SD $6$: compute z-scores for $76$ and $55$.
A distribution has mean $90$ and SD $12$. What raw value has $z = 2.5$?
Normal: mean $40$, SD $4$. What share lies between $36$ and $44$? Between $32$ and $48$?
Normal: mean $500$, SD $90$. What share exceeds $680$?
Normal exam: mean $71$, SD $7$. Scholarships go to the top $16\%$. Find the cutoff.
Ana runs $z = -1.8$ on 5K times (lower better); Bat lifts $z = +1.5$ on bench press. Who is further from typical in their sport?
practice.solution: $|{-1.8}| > |1.5|$: Ana. Direction differs by sport; distance from typical is $|z|$.

testYourself.statement: A histogram of $60$ delivery times shows bars $5, 18, 22, 10, 3$, plus a detached bar of $2$ far right. Describe shape, and name the summary pair you'd report.
testYourself.solution: Unimodal core (hump in the third bin), roughly symmetric, with $2$ detached high outliers. Sum check: $5+18+22+10+3+2 = 60$ ✓. With flagged outliers: median + IQR.

testYourself.statement: On a $25$-student exam, Tulga beat or tied $20$ students. His percentile — and is 'he scored $80\%$' the same claim?
testYourself.solution: $\frac{20}{25} \times 100 = 80$th percentile. NOT the same as scoring $80\%$: percentile is crowd position; the raw score could be anything.

testYourself.statement: Two normal exams: X (mean $60$, SD $10$) and Y (mean $75$, SD $5$). Saraa scored $78$ on X, Dulguun $83$ on Y. Standardize, compare, and translate both to percentiles.
testYourself.solution: $z_S = \frac{78-60}{10} = 1.8$; $z_D = \frac{83-75}{5} = 1.6$. Saraa stands (slightly) further up: between $P_{84}$ and $P_{97.5}$ for both, Saraa nearer the top. Same-direction bracket, decided by z.

testYourself.statement: Machine parts: normal, mean $50.0$ mm, SD $0.2$. Specs demand $49.6$–$50.4$ mm. What share of parts is out of spec — and per box of $200$, how many rejects do you expect?
testYourself.solution: Specs sit at $\mu \pm 2\sigma$: $5\%$ out of spec. Per $200$: expected $200 \times 0.05 = 10$ rejects (binomial mean — Unit 8 consuming Unit 10).

testYourself.statement: Normal blood-pressure data: mean $120$, SD $10$. A clinic wants to flag only the top $0.15\%$. Set the threshold, and explain the trade-off vs a $\mu + 2\sigma$ threshold.
testYourself.solution: Top $0.15\%$ begins at $z = +3$: threshold $150$. Trade-off: at $140$ ($z=2$) the clinic catches more true cases but falsely flags $2.5\%$ of healthy patients; at $150$ false alarms nearly vanish but real borderline cases slip through. Threshold-setting IS choosing which error to prefer.

testYourself.statement: A right-skewed income dataset has mean $4.2$M₮, SD $3$M₮. A colleague computes z = 2 for a $10.2$M₮ earner and announces "top 2.5% by the empirical rule." Name the error and the correct approach.
testYourself.solution: The z-arithmetic is right ($\frac{10.2-4.2}{3} = 2$) but the PERCENTILE claim needs the bell — incomes are right-skewed, so the z-to-percentile bridge is out. Correct: compute the percentile directly from the sorted data (the earner's at-or-below share). Shape check comes before rule use, every time.
